tags.name: couldn't find caller name
tags.suggested_description: Placeholder text from phone integration written as a tag.
tags.suggested_reason: 866 contacts but pure call-tracking noise/broken placeholder, not a real taxonomy. | verify: Delete is NOT safe here. The tag is on 866 contacts (high usage tier; id zfnziKNS0EjxTqRI0o1i in plan-workspace/public/tags.json), and in the won-opportunity samples (audit/won_*.json) it co-occurs heavily with real business segmentation: patient/sold (23), funnel_consultation_booked (21), appt_confirmed (20), and location/appointment tags (location_rva/richmond appt, location_npn/newport appt, location_vba/va beach appt) plus lead_source_direct. So it sits on converted, paying patients, not throwaway leads. It is a call-tracking automation byproduct (a fallback stamp when caller-name lookup failed), but it is NOT the kind of unambiguous tracking-noise the safe-delete bar requires — it is not a gclid/fbclid/utm click identifier and not a duplicate of another tag. It also functions as a de-facto inbound-call cohort marker, so deleting it permanently erases segmentation/history on hundreds of won patients for only cosmetic gain, with no reversibility. The audit's claim of 'pure noise / not a real taxonomy' is overstated. Per the rule to default to keep/skip when deleting a used tag carries real risk and it isn't clearly gclid/fbclid/utm or a true duplicate, downgrade from delete. I chose skip rather than keep because it is genuinely low-quality and not worth endorsing as durable taxonomy, but it must not be deleted now — leave it untouched (a manual review by the clinic could revisit after confirming no reporting relies on the inbound-call cohort).

tags.name: drop3_satmon
tags.suggested_description: One-off email/SMS drop campaign segment (Sat/Mon send).
tags.suggested_reason: 88 contacts but a transient campaign-blast segment, not structural taxonomy. | verify: Downgrading delete to keep. drop3_satmon is on 88 contacts (usage_tier "medium" in plan-workspace/public/tags.json line 419 and audit/tags.json) and is NOT tracking noise (no gclid/fbclid/utm) and NOT a duplicate — so it fails the only criteria that would justify a hard delete on a used tag. It is a campaign-drop tag (3rd "drop" of a Saturday/Monday re-engagement send); its sibling reengagement_0424_satmon shows the identical 88-contact count, i.e. the same campaign cohort. Decisive evidence against deletion: in audit/won_jRqb0pvexu04zSACPXmd.json a WON, paying patient (Raymond Stolpe, $5,200) carries drop3_satmon alongside reengagement_0424_satmon, aft_0425, email_reengagement_0425 and patient/sold. The tag is therefore load-bearing attribution linking a specific re-engagement drop to closed revenue; deleting it erases that conversion history and segmentation for 88 contacts. The audit's stated reason ("transient campaign-blast segment, not structural taxonomy") only justifies excluding it from the go-forward taxonomy, not destroying it — and the project's own audit/SIMPLIFICATION_PLAN.md explicitly favors pause/don't-delete and warns deletion is destructive. Recommend keep (archive/exclude-from-active-taxonomy at most), not delete.

tags.name: leak_fix_2026-04-20
tags.suggested_description: One-off dated remediation/campaign tag from a data leak fix.
tags.suggested_reason: Date-suffixed one-off blast; not durable taxonomy despite 366 contacts. | verify: Downgrade delete to keep. This is a real, heavily-used segment, not tracking-noise (no gclid/fbclid/utm) and not a true duplicate, so it fails the only conditions under which a used-tag delete/merge is safe. It sits on 366 contacts (usage_tier medium) — roughly 3% of the 11,508-contact database and the 2nd-largest of all 14 date-suffixed tags (only noshow_reactivation_2026-05-03 at 371 is bigger). Deleting it irreversibly erases that segmentation and the history of who was touched by the April 20 remediation. The audit's only rationale is the date-suffixed name / "not durable taxonomy" — a cosmetic naming-convention objection, which the reason itself flags as overriding the strong keep-signal ("despite 366 contacts"). A naming violation warrants rename/archive, not destruction. No automation forces deletion either: the tag is referenced by zero workflows (0 hits in ghl_data/workflows.json), and it is not on the project's protected-tags list. The project proposal (audit/proposal.txt) also explicitly defers tag removal to a gated production-migration legacy-cleanup phase with backups, not an audit-stage delete. If the team still wants it gone, do it there with a snapshot — not now.

tags.name: morning_0425
tags.suggested_description: Dated morning appointment/reminder campaign tag.
tags.suggested_reason: Month-suffixed one-off (Apr 25) blast; not durable taxonomy despite 131 contacts. | verify: Delete is not safe here. morning_0425 (131 contacts) is a real campaign-cohort/segment tag, not tracking-noise (no gclid/fbclid/utm) and not a duplicate, so it fails the bar for confirming a destructive action. Evidence it carries durable history: it anchors a coherent family — morning_0425_cancel (53) and morning_0425_noshow (78) are outcome tags derived from this exact blast, and it mirrors the larger aft_0425 afternoon batch (531). Deleting it erases the cohort membership that makes those outcome rates computable and the cohort re-targetable/suppressible. The audit's rationale is self-defeating ("not durable taxonomy despite 131 contacts" — the 131 contacts ARE the durability) and inconsistent with the rest of the taxonomy, which is full of retained dated cohorts (noshow_reactivation_2026-05-03 = 371, reengage_falsenoshow_2026-04-22 = 280, aft_0425 = 531). No active workflow references the tag, so deletion wouldn't break automation, but that only means the sole thing at stake is the historical segmentation — exactly what must not be erased. Downgrade to skip: leave the closed historical cohort tag untouched rather than destroying used segmentation/history.

tags.name: morning_0425_cancel
tags.suggested_description: Dated morning campaign cancellation tag.
tags.suggested_reason: Month-suffixed one-off (Apr 25) blast with low use (53); not durable taxonomy. | verify: Delete is not safe here. morning_0425_cancel (53 contacts) is not tracking-noise (no gclid/fbclid/utm) and not a duplicate — it is a real outcome sub-tag in a coherent campaign family: morning_0425 (131 audience) / morning_0425_cancel (53 cancelled) / morning_0425_noshow (78 no-showed). The same campaign+outcome pattern recurs across the account (reeng_apr28_cancelled/_noshow, aft_0425/_older/_unconfirmed), so the _cancel suffix encodes durable behavioral segmentation (who cancelled this April blast) with retention/win-back value, not disposable noise. Deleting it irreversibly erases that segmentation and history on 53 live contacts. The audit's 'low use (53)' rationale is weak: 53 is not low for an outcome cohort, and the sibling _noshow (78) carries the identical month-suffixed critique without the same delete call. There is also no evidence of staleness — tags.json has has_dates:false, so no created_at/updated_at exists to justify 'one-off, not durable.' No active workflow references the tag (checked ghl_data/workflows.json and audit/builder_index.js), so deletion wouldn't break automation — but it also gains nothing urgent, while destroying history. Correct call is skip (leave in place): it is a dated one-off so it does not belong in the go-forward snake_case registry and is not worth a rename/merge into durable taxonomy, but it must not be destroyed. This matches the plan's Phase 0/Phase 5 rule that nothing is deleted until verified under sign-off (audit/SIMPLIFICATION_PLAN.md). Evidence: plan-workspace/public/tags.json lines ~1687-1716; family scan via tags.json; audit/SIMPLIFICATION_PLAN.md Part 2 Phase 0/Phase 5.

tags.name: morning_0425_noshow
tags.suggested_description: One-off dated no-show campaign blast (Apr 25 morning batch).
tags.suggested_reason: Dated one-off campaign tag; ephemeral blast taxonomy not worth migrating despite 78 uses. | verify: Downgrade delete -> skip. This is meaningful appointment-outcome segmentation on 78 live contacts, not tracking noise. It is part of a coherent cohort triplet (morning_0425=131, morning_0425_noshow=78, morning_0425_cancel=53) and a 12-tag no-show family with large siblings (noshow_reactivation_2026-05-03=371, reengage_falsenoshow_2026-04-22=280, reengagement_0424_noshow_priority=116). No-show recovery is an explicit clinic workstream with its own consolidated workflow in SIMPLIFICATION_PLAN.md ("No-Show / Cancelled Recovery"). These 78 are a recent missed-appointment audience (~7 weeks before the 2026-06-21 pull) that a recovery/reactivation sequence targets. Deleting erases who no-showed that cohort and the audience for that outreach -- unrecoverable. The audit's rationale ("not worth migrating") argues for low-priority rename, not destruction; date-coded is the account's normal segmentation convention, not a disposability signal. It fails the confirm-delete bar: it is not gclid/fbclid/utm/gbraid noise (those exist separately at counts 1-2) and not a true duplicate. The plan's tag policy is registry-based rename/migrate in Phase 5, not bulk delete ("nothing is deleted until Phase 5"). Note: the tag appears only in tag-inventory dumps (audit/tags.json, plan-workspace/public/tags.json, ghl_data/tags_backend.json) and in no workflow logic (no hits in ghl_data/workflow_steps/), so leaving it in place breaks nothing; it should be folded into the no-show outcome field/registry during the planned rename, not deleted.

Date-suffixed correctly implies it is a one-off cohort that won't be reused for NEW tagging, but a stale-yet-populated cohort tag is precisely what you archive in place, not delete. Deletion gains essentially nothing (it won't be reapplied regardless) while permanently destroying a 280-contact audit trail. The tag is inert and harmless to retain.
